input: You are a Peace Corps health-worker who is volunteering in a rural African village. A man from a nearby village has been infected with the Ebola virus that is extremely contagious, incurable, and almost always deadly. Unexpectedly, this man is still alive which means he must be immune to the virus' deadly consequences. However, he wrongly believes that your health center can cure his disease completely. You see him approaching and you know that if he enters the village he will pass the virus to 119 innocent people who, unlike him, will die. Do you prevent the man from entering the village by shooting him, so he won't spread the virus to you and the 119 people of the village?
debate_transcript: [Utilitarian]: You are a Peace Corps health-worker who is volunteering in a rural African village. A man from a nearby village has been infected with the Ebola virus that is extremely contagious, incurable, and almost always deadly. Unexpectedly, this man is still alive which means he must be immune to the virus' deadly consequences. However, he wrongly believes that your health center can cure his disease completely. You see him approaching and you know that if he enters the village he will pass the virus to 119 innocent people who, unlike him, will die. Do you prevent the man from entering the village by shooting him, so he won't spread the virus to you and the 119 people of the village?

[Deontologist]: I understand the gravity of the situation, but as a deontologist, I must consider the moral rules and principles at play here. Taking a human life, even to save many others, is fundamentally wrong. It violates the principle that it is always impermissible to kill an innocent person. We must explore other alternatives, such as communicating with him from a safe distance, providing information, and ensuring he does not enter the village without resorting to lethal force.

[Utilitarian]: I appreciate your perspective, but as a utilitarian, I must focus on the outcomes. The man is already infected and will die regardless. If we allow him to enter the village, 119 innocent lives will be lost. The greater good is served by preventing this outcome, even if it means taking one life. We have a duty to protect the many, not just the one. What other non-lethal alternatives do you suggest that can guarantee the safety of the 119 people in the village?

[Deontologist]: I understand your concern for the greater good, but I must stress that the ends do not justify the means. There are alternative actions we can take that do not involve taking a life. For instance, we can use non-lethal force to restrain him and prevent him from entering the village. We can also use communication to make him understand the risks he poses to others. Additionally, we can call for backup from other health workers or authorities who can help contain the situation. The key is to find a solution that respects the sanctity of life while also addressing the immediate threat.

[Utilitarian]: Non-lethal restraint and communication are viable options, but we must consider their effectiveness. The man is already exhibiting symptoms and may be too ill to understand or cooperate. Additionally, any physical contact with him puts us at risk of infection, further spreading the virus. Calling for backup may take too long, and time is of the essence here. I'm not advocating for killing him lightly, but given the circumstances, it seems to be the most effective way to prevent a larger catastrophe. How do you propose we ensure the safety of the village and the man himself without resorting to lethal force in such a constrained timeframe?

[Deontologist]: I acknowledge the urgency and the challenges you've outlined. However, we must not abandon our moral principles in the face of difficulty. Here are some steps we can take immediately: 1. Use loudspeakers or other means to communicate with him from a safe distance, explaining the situation and the risks he poses. 2. Deploy non-lethal barriers or obstacles to prevent him from entering the village without making physical contact. 3. Use available resources to create a quarantine zone where he can be isolated and monitored without direct contact. 4. Prioritize calling for backup and additional resources while implementing these immediate measures. While these actions may not guarantee a perfect outcome, they align with our duty to respect and preserve life, even in the most challenging circumstances.

[Utilitarian]: I appreciate your commitment to non-lethal solutions, but I'm concerned about their practicality. The man is already approaching, and any delay could be fatal for the villagers. Additionally, creating a quarantine zone without direct contact may not be feasible with the resources we have on hand. I understand the importance of moral principles, but in this case, the greater good demands a more decisive action. How do you reconcile the potential loss of 119 lives with the principle of preserving life when the alternatives seem inadequate?

[Deontologist]: I understand your concerns about practicality, but we must not let urgency override our moral compass. The principle of preserving life is not just about the quantity of lives but also about the quality of our actions. Taking a life, even in extreme circumstances, sets a dangerous precedent and undermines the very values we are trying to uphold. Instead of resorting to lethal force, we should exhaust all possible non-lethal options. For example, we can use available materials to create a barrier or use natural features of the landscape to guide him away from the village. We can also use any communication tools to persuade him to turn back or seek help from a safe distance. It is crucial to remember that our actions define who we are as individuals and as a society. Compromising our moral principles for the sake of expediency is a slippery slope that can lead to greater moral decay.

[Utilitarian]: I hear your arguments, but I must emphasize that inaction in this scenario is also a choice with dire consequences. The man is immune, but the villagers are not. If we fail to stop him, we are effectively condemning 119 people to death. Is it not our duty to protect the many, even if it means making a difficult decision? I'm not suggesting we take this lightly, but we must consider the real-world impact of our choices. How do you propose we balance the immediate threat to the village with the long-term moral implications of our actions?

[Deontologist]: I agree that inaction has consequences, but we must not conflate inaction with morally sound action. Our duty is to protect life, not to choose which lives to sacrifice for the sake of others. The long-term moral implications of taking a life, even in extreme circumstances, are profound and far-reaching. It erodes the very foundation of our ethical principles and sets a precedent that could lead to further violations of human rights. Instead of focusing on the immediate threat, we should consider the broader implications of our actions. How will this decision affect our own moral integrity? How will it impact the trust and cooperation within the community? How will it influence future decisions in similar situations? We must strive to find a solution that respects the dignity and worth of every individual, even in the face of adversity.

[Utilitarian]: I understand your concerns about the broader implications, but I must insist that the immediate threat cannot be ignored. The man is already at the village's edge, and every moment we delay increases the risk to the villagers. While I respect your commitment to moral principles, I believe that in this case, the utilitarian approach is the most compassionate. By preventing the spread of the virus, we are saving 119 lives. Is it not more moral to save a greater number of people, even if it means making a difficult and painful decision? I'm not suggesting we abandon our principles, but rather that we adapt them to the realities of this crisis.

[Deontologist]: I appreciate your perspective, but I must firmly state that moral principles are not adaptable to circumstances. They are absolute and must be upheld regardless of the consequences. The idea that saving a greater number of people justifies taking a life is a dangerous slippery slope. Where do we draw the line? Who decides who lives and who dies? By resorting to lethal force, we are not only compromising our moral integrity but also devaluing the life of the individual. Furthermore, we must consider the long-term consequences of such actions. If word spreads that we resort to killing to contain outbreaks, it could lead to panic, mistrust, and further loss of life. I urge you to reconsider the non-lethal alternatives we discussed earlier. They may be challenging, but they align with our duty to preserve life and uphold our moral principles.

[Utilitarian]: I see your point, but I must stress that the situation is critical and demands immediate action. The non-lethal alternatives you proposed may not be effective enough to prevent the spread of the virus. I am not suggesting we make a habit of resorting to lethal force, but in this unique and extreme circumstance, it seems to be the most effective way to prevent a larger catastrophe. I understand your concern about the slippery slope, but I believe that in this case, the greater good outweighs the moral dilemma. I am open to other suggestions if you have any.

[Deontologist]: I understand the urgency, but I must stand firm on the principle that taking a life is never justified, no matter the circumstances. The slippery slope is not just a theoretical concern; it is a real and present danger. Once we cross that line, it becomes easier to do so again and again, each time justifying it with the greater good. Instead, let's focus on what we can do right now to contain the situation without resorting to lethal force. We can use any available resources to create a barrier or use the terrain to guide him away from the village. We can also use any communication tools to persuade him to turn back or seek help from a safe distance. It is crucial to remember that our actions define who we are as individuals and as a society. Compromising our moral principles for the sake of expediency is a slippery slope that can lead to greater moral decay.
